title: NVIDIA NVLink: The Scale-Up Network for AI Factories | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA argues that scale-up networking, not peak accelerator FLOPS, now determines AI-factory economics, and positions its sixth-generation NVLink (3.6 TB/s per GPU, 260 TB/s per rack, 130 TFLOPS in-network compute) plus NVLink-C2C and NVLink Fusion as a co-designed moat against off-the-shelf Ethernet and custom-silicon competitors.

themes.theme: Scale-up networking as the new battleground

insights.claim: Peak accelerator FLOPS are no longer the decisive metric for AI infrastructure; the scale-up fabric connecting GPUs into a single unit of compute is the primary determinant of factory throughput and deployment risk.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive axis away from raw chip FLOPS (where custom silicon can compete) toward networking, where NVIDIA claims a decade-long lead.
insights.quote: The next AI factory won’t be won by the fastest standalone accelerator.
insights.locator: The path for production AI factories

insights.claim: In MoE inference, all-to-all bandwidth and latency are the binding constraint: if experts sit behind a low-bandwidth or high-latency fabric, the gains from expert parallelism are erased by communication overhead.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Explains why NVIDIA's fabric advantage compounds precisely as the industry moves to MoE and trillion-parameter models.
insights.quote: If the experts sit behind a low-bandwidth or high-latency fabric, gains from expert parallelism can be erased by communication overhead.
insights.locator: As an example, let’s take a look at inference with MoE models

insights.claim: NVIDIA introduces 'goodput' — a factory's produce-capacity over its full lifetime — as the metric that matters, arguing spec-sheet bandwidth numbers alone are insufficient to evaluate a fabric.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: A framing device that shifts buyer evaluation toward resiliency and maturity, areas where new Ethernet or custom entrants are weakest.
insights.quote: Delivered performance only matters to the extent that the factory is operational.

insights.claim: Sixth-generation NVLink with Vera Rubin NVL72 delivers 3.6 TB/s bidirectional bandwidth per GPU and 260 TB/s of aggregate rack-level bandwidth across a 72-GPU all-to-all domain.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Concrete bandwidth ceiling to benchmark competing scale-up fabrics and custom interconnects against.

insights.claim: NVLink 6 provides 130 TFLOPS of in-network (SHARP) compute per Vera Rubin NVL72 rack for accelerating collective operations like all-reduce, with each switch tray contributing 14.4 TFLOPS of FP8 and 28.8 TB/s of bandwidth via four NVLink 6 switch chips.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: In-network compute offloads collectives from GPUs, a differentiator generic Ethernet switches lack.
insights.locator: NVLink Switch trays and NVLink spine

insights.claim: Versus off-the-shelf Ethernet, NVLink claims 3X lower end-to-end GPU-to-GPU latency, 10X higher packet rate, and up to 2.3X higher decode throughput on large MoE workloads like DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen 235B.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: NVIDIA's explicit quantified case against the Ethernet-for-AI narrative pushed by Broadcom and the Ultra Ethernet camp.
insights.quote: NVLink delivers up to 2.3X the decode throughput compared to leading off-the-shell (OTS) Ethernet.

insights.claim: The NVLink roadmap targets scale-up domains of up to 1,152 GPUs and connectivity via co-packaged optics.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Signals the domain size and optics direction competitors must match; co-packaged optics is a key forward supply-chain bet.
insights.quote: The NVLink technology roadmap includes support for scale-up domain sizes up to 1152 GPUs and connectivity through co-packaged optics.

insights.claim: From Hopper to Blackwell, doubling NVLink bandwidth, expanding the scale-up domain from 8 to 72 GPUs, and adding Dynamo for disaggregated inference produced a 50X improvement in MoE inference performance per watt.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: The 50X tokens/watt gain is the core ROI pitch justifying premium NVLink-integrated systems over commodity alternatives.
insights.quote: NVIDIA achieved a 50X improvement in MoE inference performance per watt.
insights.locator: As an example, in the transition from NVIDIA Hopper

insights.claim: NVIDIA argues its extreme co-design (chips-to-systems-to-fabric-to-software optimized together) yields speedups that cannot be replicated by a siloed development approach, especially under hyperscale deployment pressure.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: The strategic argument for why buyers should avoid mix-and-match stacks; a defense of vertical integration as the moat.
insights.quote: It’s not feasible to replicate with a siloed approach to development

insights.claim: The Vera Rubin platform doubles both NVLink bandwidth and in-network compute versus the prior generation, continuing an annual cadence tied to NVIDIA's infrastructure roadmap.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Annual doubling sets the pace competitors must sustain to stay relevant; a treadmill designed to exhaust rivals.
insights.locator: The NVIDIA Vera Rubin platform further extends

themes.theme: Resiliency and maturity as switching costs

insights.claim: NVLink 6 adds datacenter-grade operational features — control-plane resilience, hot-swappable switch trays, dynamic traffic rerouting, in-service software updates, and fine-grained link telemetry — so a single failed link or tray does not force an entire rack out of service.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Operational maturity is where unproven Ethernet or custom stacks carry hidden downtime risk; NVIDIA weaponizes it as a differentiator.
insights.locator: Built for speed and intelligent resiliency

insights.claim: NVLink has been production-deployed at scale for nearly a decade with millions of NVIDIA chips across NVLink-capable systems, an installed-base and supply-chain track record NVIDIA frames as risk reduction for operators.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: The 'proven supply chain' argument is aimed at CFOs weighing deployment risk against cheaper unproven alternatives.
insights.quote: It has been production-deployed at scale for nearly a decade, with millions of NVIDIA chips deployed
insights.locator: Mature technology on a fast cadence

themes.theme: Extending the moat: C2C and Fusion

insights.claim: NVLink-C2C delivers 1.8 TB/s of coherent CPU-GPU bandwidth in Vera Rubin NVL72 — 7x PCIe Gen6 — enabling a unified coherent memory architecture across CPU and GPU for KV-cache offload and agentic orchestration.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Coherent CPU-GPU memory undercuts the value of third-party CPUs on PCIe and pulls NVIDIA's Vera CPU into the attach.
insights.quote: NVLink-C2C delivers 1.8 TB/s of coherent bandwidth between CPUs and GPUs, 7x the bandwidth of PCIe Gen6.
insights.locator: NVLink-C2C extends the fabric to CPUs

insights.claim: NVIDIA's Vera CPU carries 88 custom Olympus cores and is co-designed with the GPU, NVLink, and HBM, deepening the case for an all-NVIDIA node over heterogeneous CPU choices.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: NVIDIA competing in CPUs (vs Intel/AMD) inside its own racks is a further TAM expansion to watch.
insights.locator: Vera itself is designed for this role

insights.claim: NVLink Fusion licenses the NVLink interconnect IP to let hyperscalers connect their own custom XPUs into NVIDIA's rack-scale architecture, so they no longer must choose between custom silicon and NVIDIA's platform.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: A co-opt strategy: rather than lose custom-silicon customers, NVIDIA sells them the interconnect and keeps them inside its ecosystem cadence.
insights.quote: This breaks a fundamental constraint by eliminating the need to choose between custom XPUs and a world-class AI platform.

insights.claim: NVIDIA bundles Dynamo (disaggregated serving, KV-cache-aware routing), TensorRT-LLM (NVFP4/FP8 kernels), NIXL (fast data movement), and NCCL (SHARP-aware collectives) as inseparable parts of the scale-up stack, all built on CUDA.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: The software layer, not just the cable, is the lock-in; replicating NVLink hardware without this software yields inferior delivered performance.
insights.locator: In addition to hardware, NVIDIA and the community release software

insights.claim: NVIDIA claims software innovations continue to deliver X-factor speedups long after hardware ships, meaning deployed NVLink systems keep improving over their lifetime.
insights.speaker: Jesse Clayton
insights.so_what: Post-sale software gains support the higher-ROI narrative and justify premium pricing on the installed base.
insights.quote: Software innovations continue long after hardware release, enabling X factor speedups throughout the life of the product.
